examples.title: Algorithm: Stack Operations (LIFO)
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating basic stack operations following the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle
examples.code:
ALGORITHM StackOperations
// Stack ADT implementation showing LIFO principle
INPUT: An integer value to push onto the stack
OUTPUT: The popped value or error message

DATA STRUCTURE:
 Stack: array of MAX elements
 top: integer initialized to -1

// ---------- INITIALIZE STACK ----------
FUNCTION InitStack()
 top ← -1
 RETURN Stack
END FUNCTION

// ---------- CHECK IF EMPTY ----------
FUNCTION IsEmpty(Stack)
 IF top = -1 THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END FUNCTION

// ---------- CHECK IF FULL ----------
FUNCTION IsFull(Stack)
 IF top = MAX - 1 THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END FUNCTION

// ---------- PUSH OPERATION ----------
FUNCTION Push(Stack, value)
 IF IsFull(Stack) THEN
 PRINT "Stack Overflow! Cannot push " + value
 RETURN
 END IF
 top ← top + 1
 Stack[top] ← value
 PRINT "Pushed " + value + " to stack"
END FUNCTION

// ---------- POP OPERATION ----------
FUNCTION Pop(Stack)
 IF IsEmpty(Stack) THEN
 PRINT "Stack Underflow! Cannot pop"
 RETURN NULL // or error value
 END IF
 value ← Stack[top]
 top ← top - 1
 RETURN value
END FUNCTION

// ---------- PEEK OPERATION ----------
FUNCTION Peek(Stack)
 IF IsEmpty(Stack) THEN
 PRINT "Stack is empty"
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 RETURN Stack[top]
END FUNCTION

// ---------- DISPLAY STACK ----------
FUNCTION Display(Stack)
 IF IsEmpty(Stack) THEN
 PRINT "Stack is empty"
 RETURN
 END IF
 PRINT "Stack contents (top to bottom): "
 FOR i ← top DOWN TO 0 DO
 PRINT Stack[i] + " "
 END FOR
 PRINT newline
END FUNCTION

// ---------- MAIN DEMONSTRATION ----------
BEGIN
 Stack ← InitStack()
 
 // Demonstrate LIFO: Last In, First Out
 Push(Stack, 10) // Stack: [10]
 Push(Stack, 20) // Stack: [10, 20]
 Push(Stack, 30) // Stack: [10, 20, 30] <- top
 
 Display(Stack) // Output: 30 20 10
 
 PRINT "Peek: " + Peek(Stack) // Output: 30
 
 PRINT "Pop: " + Pop(Stack) // Output: 30 (last element removed)
 PRINT "Pop: " + Pop(Stack) // Output: 20 (second element now)
 
 Display(Stack) // Output: 10
 
 // Verify LIFO: 30 was pushed last, but removed first!
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the fundamental stack operations. The key concept is the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle where elements are added to and removed from the same end called 'top'. The top variable starts at -1 (empty stack), increments on push, and decrements on pop. Overflow occurs when top reaches MAX-1, and underflow occurs when attempting to pop from an empty stack (top = -1). All operations are O(1) constant time.

examples.title: Python: Stack Implementation with Class
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of stack data structure using a class with all ADT operations
examples.code:
"""
Stack Implementation in Python
Demonstrates LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle
2022 Scheme - Data Structures Topic
"""

class Stack:
 """Stack ADT implementation using Python list"""
 
 def __init__(self, max_size=100):
 """Initialize empty stack with maximum size"""
 self.stack = []
 self.max_size = max_size
 self.top = -1 # -1 indicates empty stack
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if stack is empty - O(1)"""
 return self.top == -1
 
 def is_full(self):
 """Check if stack is full - O(1)"""
 return self.top == self.max_size - 1
 
 def push(self, item):
 """Push item onto top of stack - O(1)"""
 if self.is_full():
 print(f"Stack Overflow! Cannot push {item}")
 return False
 self.stack.append(item)
 self.top += 1
 print(f"Pushed {item} to stack")
 return True
 
 def pop(self):
 """Remove and return top element - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Stack Underflow! Cannot pop")
 return None
 item = self.stack.pop()
 self.top -= 1
 return item
 
 def peek(self):
 """Return top element without removing - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Stack is empty!")
 return None
 return self.stack[self.top]
 
 def size(self):
 """Return current number of elements - O(1)"""
 return self.top + 1
 
 def display(self):
 """Display stack contents from top to bottom"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Stack is empty.")
 return
 print("Stack (top to bottom):", end=" ")
 for i in range(self.top, -1, -1):
 print(self.stack[i], end=" ")
 print()


# Demonstration of LIFO Principle
def main():
 print("=" * 50)
 print("STACK DEMONSTRATION - LIFO Principle")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 s = Stack(max_size=10)
 
 # Push elements
 print("\n--- Pushing elements ---")
 s.push(10)
 s.push(20)
 s.push(30)
 
 # Display current stack
 s.display() # Output: 30 20 10
 
 # Peek at top element
 print(f"\nPeek: {s.peek()}") # Output: 30
 
 # Pop elements - demonstrates LIFO
 print("\n--- Popping elements (LIFO) ---")
 print(f"Popped: {s.pop()}") # Output: 30 (last in, first out)
 print(f"Popped: {s.pop()}") # Output: 20
 print(f"Popped: {s.pop()}") # Output: 10
 
 s.display() # Stack is empty
 
 # Test underflow
 print("\n--- Testing Underflow ---")
 s.pop() # Should print Stack Underflow
 
 # Test overflow
 print("\n--- Testing Overflow ---")
 small_stack = Stack(max_size=3)
 small_stack.push(1)
 small_stack.push(2)
 small_stack.push(3)
 small_stack.push(4) # Should print Stack Overflow


if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()

# ==================== Application: String Reversal ====================
def reverse_string(s):
 """Reverse a string using stack - O(n)"""
 stack = Stack()
 
 # Push all characters
 for char in s:
 stack.push(char)
 
 # Pop to reverse
 reversed_str = ""
 while not stack.is_empty():
 reversed_str += stack.pop()
 
 return reversed_str


def reverse_demo():
 """Demonstrate string reversal using stack"""
 original = "CSE"
 reversed_str = reverse_string(original)
 print(f"Original: {original}")
 print(f"Reversed: {reversed_str}")


# Run reverse demo
print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("STRING REVERSAL USING STACK")
print("=" * 50)
reverse_demo()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation shows a complete stack class with all standard operations. The stack uses Python's list internally with a top pointer. Key points: push/pop/peek are all O(1) constant time operations. The demonstration shows the LIFO principle clearly - elements pushed as 10, 20, 30 are popped as 30, 20, 10. The reverse_string function demonstrates an application of stacks where characters are pushed and popped to reverse the order.
examples.timeComplexity: push: O(1), pop: O(1), peek: O(1), isEmpty: O(1), isFull: O(1)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(max_size) - fixed array allocation, O(n) for actual elements stored

examples.title: C: Stack Implementation using Struct
examples.description: C implementation of stack using struct approach with all operations following LIFO principle
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the struct-based approach for stack implementation as preferred in the the syllabus. Key features: 1) Uses a struct with array and top pointer, 2) All operations (push, pop, peek, isEmpty, isFull) are O(1), 3) Properly handles overflow and underflow conditions, 4) Includes practical applications like string reversal and parenthesis matching. The LIFO principle is clearly shown: pushing 10, 20, 30 and then popping gives 30, 20, 10.
examples.timeComplexity: push: O(1), pop: O(1), peek: O(1), isEmpty: O(1), isFull: O(1)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(MAX) for fixed array allocation, where MAX=100

examples.title: Java: Stack Class Implementation
examples.description: Java implementation of stack using arrays with exception handling and typical Java idioms
examples.explanation: This Java implementation shows a complete stack class with typical Java exception handling. Key points: 1) Uses array with top pointer, 2) Implements Stack ADT with push, pop, peek, isEmpty, isFull operations, 3) All operations are O(1), 4) Includes applications like string reversal and parenthesis matching, 5) Uses Java's EmptyStackException for error handling. The LIFO principle is demonstrated: pushing 10, 20, 30 results in popping 30, 20, 10. Note that Java also provides a built-in Stack class in java.util which extends Vector.
examples.timeComplexity: push: O(1), pop: O(1), peek: O(1), isEmpty: O(1), isFull: O(1)
